content:
# Genesis Chapter 47 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 27v–28r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_047_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_047_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0051 (page 47), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0052 (page 48)

---

## Five Brothers Before Pharaoh

**1** Joseph went in and told Pharaoh: "My father and my brothers have come from the land of Canaan, with their flocks and their herds and everything they own. They are now in the land of Goshen." **2** From the full complement of his brothers he took five men and presented them before Pharaoh.

**3** Pharaoh said to his brothers, "What is your occupation?" And they said to Pharaoh, "Your servants are shepherds — we and our fathers before us." **4** They said to Pharaoh, "We have come to sojourn in the land, because the famine is severe in the land of Canaan and there is no pasture for your servants' flocks. Please let your servants settle in the land of Goshen."

**5** Pharaoh said to Joseph, "Your father and your brothers have come to you. **6** The land of Egypt is before you — settle your father and your brothers in the best of the land. Let them live in the land of Goshen. And if you know any capable men among them, put them in charge of my own livestock."

---

## Jacob Meets Pharaoh

**7** Then Joseph brought his father Jacob in and presented him before Pharaoh. And Jacob blessed Pharaoh. **8** Pharaoh said to Jacob, "How many are the days of the years of your life?" **9** Jacob said to Pharaoh, "The days of the years of my sojournings are one hundred and thirty years. Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life — they have not matched the days of the years of my fathers' lives in their sojournings." **10** Then Jacob blessed Pharaoh and went out from before Pharaoh.

---

## The Settlement in Goshen

**11** Joseph settled his father and his brothers and gave them a holding in the land of Egypt — in the best of the land, in the region of Rameses — just as Pharaoh had commanded. **12** Joseph provided food for his father and his brothers and for all his father's household, according to the number of their dependents.

---

## The Famine Economy: Joseph and the Land of Egypt

**13** Now there was no bread in all the land, because the famine was extremely severe. The land of Egypt and the land of Canaan were wasting away under the famine. **14** Joseph collected all the money that was to be found in the land of Egypt and in the land of Canaan in exchange for the grain they were buying. And Joseph brought the money into Pharaoh's house.

**15** When the money ran out in the land of Egypt and in the land of Canaan, all the Egyptians came to Joseph, saying, "Give us bread! Why should we die before your eyes? The money is gone." **16** Joseph said, "Hand over your livestock, and I will give you bread in exchange for your livestock — since the money is gone." **17** So they brought their livestock to Joseph, and Joseph gave them bread in exchange for the horses, the flocks of sheep, the herds of cattle, and the donkeys. He got them through that year with bread in exchange for all their livestock.

**18** When that year ended, they came to him the following year and said to him, "We will not hide it from our lord: the money is spent and the livestock herds belong to our lord — there is nothing left before our lord but our bodies and our land. **19** Why should we die before your eyes — we and our land together? Buy us and our land in exchange for bread, and we and our land will be servants to Pharaoh. Give us seed so that we may live and not die, and so that the land does not become desolate."

**20** So Joseph bought all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh, because the Egyptians sold their fields — every one of them — since the famine was overpowering them. And the land became Pharaoh's. **21** As for the people, he moved them into the cities, from one end of Egypt's territory to the other. **22** Only the land of the priests he did not buy, because the priests had a fixed allotment from Pharaoh, and they ate from their allotment which Pharaoh gave them. That is why they did not sell their land.

**23** Joseph said to the people, "Look — I have today bought you and your land for Pharaoh. Here is seed for you: sow the land. **24** And when the harvests come in, you will give a fifth of it to Pharaoh. The other four parts will be yours — as seed for the field and as food for yourselves and for those in your households and for feeding your little ones."

**25** They said, "You have saved our lives! May we find favor in our lord's eyes — we will be servants to Pharaoh." **26** And Joseph made it a binding statute — valid to this day over the land of Egypt — that Pharaoh should receive a fifth. Only the land of the priests did not become Pharaoh's.

---

## Israel Thrives in Goshen

**27** Israel settled in the land of Egypt, in the region of Goshen. They acquired holdings there, and they were fruitful and multiplied greatly.

---

## Jacob's Final Years and the Deathbed Oath

**28** Jacob lived in the land of Egypt for seventeen years. The total years of Jacob's life were one hundred and forty-seven years. **29** When the time drew near for Israel to die, he called for his son Joseph and said to him, "If I have truly found favor in your eyes, put your hand under my thigh and deal with me in covenant-faithfulness and truth — do not bury me in Egypt. **30** When I lie down with my fathers, carry me out of Egypt and bury me in their burial place." He said, "I will do what you have said." **31** And he said, "Swear to me." So he swore to him. And Israel bowed in worship at the head of the bed.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### "Few and Evil" — Jacob's Self-Assessment at the Court of the World

**מְעַט וְרָעִים הָיוּ יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיַּי** — "Few and evil have been the days of the years of my life."

This is one of the most striking sentences any patriarch utters in the entire book of Genesis, and its setting makes it even more remarkable: Jacob says it standing before Pharaoh — the most powerful ruler on earth, at the apex of the ancient world's most sophisticated civilization. The scene is extraordinary in itself. A weather-beaten, limping, 130-year-old shepherd from Canaan stands in the throne room of the god-king of Egypt, and when Pharaoh asks the ordinary courtly question — *How old are you?* — Jacob does not offer the diplomatic pleasantry one might expect. He speaks honestly, even bleakly, about his life.

The word **מְעַט** (*me'at*, "few") is comparatively precise: Abraham lived to 175, Isaac to 180 — the great patriarchs of the previous generation logged astonishing ages. Jacob at 130 is literally fewer years than either of his forebears at the point of death. But the word **רָעִים** (*ra'im*, "evil") is the more arresting term. *Ra'im* is the plural of *ra'* — the same word used throughout Genesis for what is "bad, evil, harmful, troubling." Jacob does not say his years were *difficult* (a softer word might serve that), or *hard*, or *wearisome*. He says they were **evil**. His own verdict on his life's days is the vocabulary of moral catastrophe.

What could justify that? The life of Jacob is a catalogue of trauma: he fled from his brother Esau in fear for his life after the stolen blessing. He was deceived by his father-in-law Laban, working fourteen years for the wife he loved after being given the wrong one on his wedding night. His daughter Dinah was violated at Shechem. His beloved wife Rachel died on the road in childbirth. He was told his favorite son Joseph was torn to pieces by a wild animal — and lived in that grief for over twenty years. His son Simeon was taken hostage in Egypt, and then he was pressured to send Benjamin as a condition of getting food. And now, at 130, he stands in Egypt — not in Canaan, not near the burial cave of his fathers, but in a foreign land, displaced by famine. "Few and evil."

Yet the verse does not end there, and neither does Jacob. He uses the noun **מְגוּרִים** (*megurim*, "sojournings") — from the same root as *ger*, the stranger or resident alien, the person who lives in a land not his own. Jacob's entire life, even in Canaan, has been a sojourning: a camping in the land of promise without yet possessing it. And the same word **מְגוּרִים** was used in God's promise to Abraham (Genesis 17:8: "the land of your sojournings"), and will appear again in the genealogical formulas throughout Genesis. To be a patriarch is to be a sojourner. Jacob is not complaining against God in some ultimate sense — he is narrating the texture of the sojourning life: it is few, and it has been hard.

The bitter precision of Jacob's self-assessment is also a kind of honesty that the narrative has been building toward. In Genesis 29, Jacob deceived his father to steal the blessing. In subsequent chapters he was himself deceived, suffered, and was shaped. By the time he stands before Pharaoh, Jacob's capacity for flattery or self-promotion appears to have been burned away entirely. He tells the most powerful man alive the plain and unflattering truth about his own life. This is a kind of integrity. The patriarchal sojourning life cannot be dressed up for the court.

Framing the scene further: the chapter opens with Jacob blessing Pharaoh (v. 7) and closes the encounter with Jacob blessing Pharaoh again (v. 10). A homeless refugee blesses the most powerful man on earth — twice. The Hebrew verb used, **וַיְבָרֶךְ** (*vayevarekh*), is the standard term for blessing, carrying full covenantal weight. The blessing of the lesser by the greater is expected — Pharaoh might bless his subjects. But here the direction is reversed: the patriarch, the covenant-carrier, blesses the emperor. The writer of Hebrews (7:7) would later observe that "the lesser is blessed by the greater" — but this scene inverts even that axiom. Jacob's blessing of Pharaoh is one of the quietly revolutionary moments in the patriarchal narratives: the man of God, even in his ruin, blesses the man of power.

---

### The Famine Economy — Savior or Pharaonic Instrument?

The twelve verses of Genesis 47:13–26 are among the most morally complex in all of Genesis, and the text's refusal to editorialize is itself a significant interpretive act.

What Joseph does, in stages, is total:

**Stage 1 (vv. 13–14):** He collects all the money in Egypt and Canaan in exchange for grain. All of it — **אֶת-כָּל-הַכֶּסֶף** (*et-kol-hakesef*, "all the silver/money"). Every coin that existed in two entire countries passes into Pharaoh's treasury. Joseph does not merely tax; he clears.

**Stage 2 (vv. 15–17):** When the money is gone, he takes all the livestock — horses, sheep, cattle, donkeys — in exchange for continued bread rations. The livestock of Egypt, which represented the agricultural productive capacity of the land, changes hands. After one more year, Pharaoh owns everything that moves.

**Stage 3 (vv. 18–22):** When the livestock is gone, the people offer themselves and their land. They say: **קְנֵה-אֹתָנוּ וְאֶת-אַדְמָתֵנוּ** (*keneh-otanu ve'et-admatenu*, "buy us and our land"). Joseph does exactly that. He buys the people and all the arable land of Egypt for Pharaoh. He then moves the population into cities — **הֶעֱבִיר אֹתוֹ לֶעָרִים** (*he'evir oto le'arim*, "he relocated them to the cities") — a massive forced population transfer, or perhaps a resettlement for administrative grain distribution.

**Stage 4 (vv. 23–26):** He returns people to the land as tenant farmers, instituting a permanent 20% tax: **וְהָיָה בַתְּבוּאֹת וּנְתַתֶּם חֲמִישִׁית לְפַרְעֹה** (*vehayah ba-tevu'ot u-netatetem hamishit le-Pharaoh*, "when the yields come in, give a fifth to Pharaoh"). The priestly class is exempt — their lands and incomes remain their own, a detail the text mentions twice (vv. 22 and 26), emphasizing that the only institution that survives the economic consolidation intact is the religious establishment.

The people's response in verse 25 is not resistance or resentment — it is gratitude: **הֶחֱיִתָנוּ** (*heheyitanu*, "you have given us life, you have kept us alive"). They call Joseph their savior. And he has, in the most literal sense, saved their biological lives. Without his famine management — the seven years of storage, the distribution system — mass death would have followed.

But the text invites the reader to hold both truths simultaneously. The man who saves lives is the same man who delivers an entire population into permanent serfdom, transferring all their wealth, their animals, their land, and their persons to Pharaoh. He creates, systematically and completely, a total state. And this is done by a Hebrew — by a son of Israel, the forefather of the nation that God is forming — in service of an Egyptian imperial system that will, within two generations, enslave that same nation.

The irony is architectural. In Genesis 47, Israel thrives in Goshen while Egypt is reduced to tenants on Pharaoh's land. But the book of Exodus opens with a Pharaoh "who did not know Joseph" (Exodus 1:8) turning this exact system — the total state, the population-control apparatus — against the Hebrews. The machine Joseph built will be used against his descendants. The text does not say this in Genesis 47. It waits for the reader to remember.

---

### "They Were Fruitful and Multiplied Greatly" — the Echo of Creation

**וַיִּרְבּוּ וַיִּפְרוּ מְאֹד** — "and they multiplied and were fruitful greatly" (v. 27).

This is not merely a census report. The vocabulary is the vocabulary of creation. In Genesis 1:28, God blesses humanity: **פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ** (*peru u-revu*, "be fruitful and multiply"). The same verbal pair — *para* (to be fruitful) and *rava* (to multiply, increase) — appears at the creation of sea creatures (Genesis 1:22) and at the blessing of humans (1:28), and then again in the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:1). These verbs are the signature of divine blessing, the fulfillment of the creational commission.

That these verbs appear now — in Egypt, in Goshen, at the very moment when the rest of Egypt has been reduced to serfdom — is deliberate and theologically significant. While Egypt is losing its land, its animals, and its freedom, Israel in Goshen is *fruitful and multiplying*. The blessing of Abraham (Genesis 12:2: "I will make you a great nation"), repeated to Isaac and Jacob, is being fulfilled not in Canaan but in Egypt, not under conditions of peace and security but under famine. The great nation is being formed in the unlikeliest of places.

The word **מְאֹד** (*me'od*, "greatly, exceedingly, very much") intensifies both verbs. They were fruitful — *greatly*. They multiplied — *greatly*. The emphasis is nearly excessive, as if the narrator wants to ensure we understand: this is the explosion of blessing that God has been promising since Abraham. It is beginning in Egypt. It will require an Exodus to get it back to the land. But it is real. It is happening. Israel is becoming a people.

The reader of Exodus will encounter this verse again, differently framed. Exodus 1:7 opens: "But the sons of Israel were fruitful and increased greatly (*vayishretsu vayirbu*) and multiplied and became exceedingly strong — and the land was filled with them." The same vocabulary, amplified. What Genesis 47:27 notes quietly — fruitful, multiply — Exodus 1:7 blasts at full volume. The two verses are literary twins, separated by the seam between books. The fruitfulness that is promise in Genesis becomes the threat that triggers the Exodus in Egypt's reckoning.

---

### *Chesed ve-Emet* — the Covenant Formula at the Deathbed

**עֲשֵׂה-עִמָּדִי חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת** — "Deal with me in covenant-faithfulness and truth" (v. 29).

The phrase **חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת** (*chesed ve-emet*, "steadfast love and truth," or as we render it, "covenant-faithfulness and truth") is one of the richest compound formulas in the Hebrew Bible. It appears first in Genesis 24:49, where Abraham's servant asks Laban and Rebekah whether they will deal in *chesed ve-emet* — faithfulness and truth — in the matter of the marriage covenant for Isaac. It appears again in Genesis 32:10, where Jacob himself uses the phrase in prayer: "I am not worthy of the least of all the *chesed* and the *emet* you have shown your servant." It will appear in Exodus 34:6 in the great theophany where God declares the divine attributes before Moses: "The LORD, the LORD, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in *chesed ve-emet*."

The formula is a covenant oath-formula. To deal in *chesed ve-emet* is not merely to be kind and honest (though it includes both) — it is to act in fidelity to the relational obligations of covenant. *Chesed* is the commitment that does not waver even when circumstances make wavering convenient. *Emet* is the reliability and truthfulness that makes a promise a promise rather than a wish. Together, the compound means something like: *Show me the faithfulness that a bound relationship demands, the kind that does not look for exits.*

Jacob uses this formula now, at the threshold of death, in asking Joseph not to bury him in Egypt. The request itself is laden with covenant weight: the burial places of the patriarchs in Canaan — the cave of Machpelah at Hebron, purchased by Abraham for Sarah (Genesis 23), where Abraham and Sarah, Isaac and Rebekah, and Leah are buried — are the tangible tokens of the covenant's territorial promise. To be buried in Egypt would be to remain in Egypt forever, at least in body. Jacob's insistence on being carried back to Canaan for burial is an act of faith in the promise — a vote, from beyond the grave, for the land God swore to give.

The pathos here is not lost: this is the man who struggled with the angel at the Jabbok, who wrenched a blessing from God by sheer refusal to let go, who grieved so long for Joseph that "he refused to be comforted" — and who now, near the end, invokes the covenant formula that he himself did not always fulfill. Jacob's *chesed* was uneven: he deceived his father, he played favorites among his sons, he withheld Benjamin in grief that nearly cost Simeon his life. But he knows what *chesed ve-emet* is. And he asks for it now — not from God but from his son — with the solemnity of an oath and the gesture of the patriarchal covenant: **שִׂים-נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי** (*sim-na yadkha tahat yerekhi*, "put your hand under my thigh").

The gesture — hand under the thigh — is the same oath-gesture used by Abraham when he swore his servant to find a wife for Isaac (Genesis 24:2). It is the most solemn physical form of the oath in Genesis, binding the one who swears not just by his word but by the generative continuity of the family line, the seed of Abraham, the future that is promised but not yet seen. Jacob asks Joseph to swear *by the covenant itself*, by the ongoing life of Israel. And Joseph swears.

---

### The Bed, the Staff, and Hebrews 11:21 — the Most Famous Variant in the Torah

**וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ יִשְׂרָאֵל עַל-רֹאשׁ הַמִּטָּה** — "And Israel bowed in worship at the head of the bed."

This final verse of Genesis 47 contains one of the most discussed textual divergences in the entire Torah, and it is a divergence of a single vowel — or rather, of the absence of vowels in the consonantal text.

The Hebrew consonantal text reads: **ראש המטה** (*rosh ha-mattah/mittah*). The word **מטה** in the consonantal text without vowel-pointing can be read in two ways:

- **מִטָּה** (*mittah*) — "bed, sleeping-couch." With this pointing (chosen by the Masoretes and preserved in the MT), Israel bows at the head of the *bed*: an old, dying man performing an act of worship at the headboard of his deathbed. He is already too weak to stand; he bows in place.

- **מַטֶּה** (*matteh*) — "staff, rod, tribe-branch." With this reading, Israel bows leaning on the top of his *staff*: an old, still-standing patriarch who steadies himself on his walking stick and bows toward the foot of the bed (or simply bows down).

The Leningrad Codex, with the Masoretic pointing fully in place, reads **מִטָּה** (mittah) — the bed. Jacob bows at the head of his bed. The image is of a man so near death that he cannot rise; he worships from his dying position, turning to bow at the headboard.

The Septuagint, translating from an older consonantal text that apparently lacked or read differently, reads **ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τῆς ῥάβδου αὐτοῦ** — "at the top of his *staff*" (*rhabdou*, rod/staff). The LXX translator read **מַטֶּה** (*matteh*, staff), not **מִטָּה** (*mittah*, bed). With this reading, Israel bows supported on his staff — the gesture of a man still standing, still dignified, worshipping on his feet.

Both readings are grammatically and narratively coherent. Both produce a plausible scene. And the variant matters enormously because the Letter to the Hebrews cites this verse as a demonstration of patriarchal faith. Hebrews 11:21 reads: "By faith Jacob, when dying, blessed each of the sons of Joseph, bowing in worship **over the top of his staff** (*epi to akron tes rhabdou autou*)." The writer of Hebrews — working from the LXX — draws a portrait of Jacob standing upright to the last, staff in hand, bowing in faith even as death approaches. It is an image of dignity and perseverance: the old wrestler, who walked with a limp since the Jabbok, leaning on his staff at the end and bowing to God.

The MT's image is different but equally moving: Jacob so weak he cannot leave his bed, yet turning at the headboard to bow in worship. Not standing — but still worshipping. The two images each carry their own theological freight.

The interesting further irony is that the author of Hebrews is citing Genesis 47:31 as evidence for something that actually occurs in Genesis 48 — Jacob's blessing of Ephraim and Manasseh. The deathbed oath of chapter 47 and the blessing of chapter 48 are closely linked narratively, but the Hebrews citation applies the staff-worship of 47:31 to the blessing of 48. This suggests the early Christian reader of the LXX understood chapters 47 and 48 as a continuous deathbed scene, with the oath and the blessing flowing together as one act of patriarchal faith at the end of Jacob's life.

Neither the MT's "bed" nor the LXX's "staff" is a corruption. They represent two ways of reading the same consonantal text — a text that was, before the vowel-pointing traditions solidified, genuinely ambiguous. The ambiguity itself is a gift: we receive two images of Jacob at the threshold of death, and both are true to the man we have known through fifty chapters.

---

## Manuscript Observations and LXX Variants

**Vaticanus page structure:** The Vaticanus image at pa_0047 (GEN_047_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) opens with the ΕΞΟΔΟC (Exodus) book-heading at the top of the page, with the large illuminated decorative initial **T** in red and gold — confirming that pa_0047 is in fact the Exodus opening page, and that Genesis 47 itself is carried on pa_0048 (GEN_047_vaticanus_page_002.jpg). The pa_0048 page shows Genesis 47 in the characteristic Vaticanus three-column uncial layout, with the dense famine-economy section (vv. 13–26) visible across two columns. A marginal chapter-number annotation appears at the right margin of pa_0048, likely a later addition by a corrector, marking the beginning of a new chapter in a Byzantine-era chapter system. Page pa_0047 thus forms the seam between Genesis and Exodus in this manuscript — the two books sharing a single opening, separated only by the ΕΞΟΔΟC heading and the illuminated initial.

**"Few and evil" (v. 9):** For **מְעַט וְרָעִים הָיוּ יְמֵי שְׁנֵי חַיַּי** (*me'at ve-ra'im hayu yemei shenei hayai*, "few and evil have been the days of the years of my life"), the LXX reads **μικραὶ καὶ πονηραί** — "small (few) and *wicked* (evil, toilsome)." The Greek **πονηρός** (*poneros*) carries a range meaning "evil, bad, harmful, toilsome" — the same word used in the New Testament for "the evil one" (Matthew 6:13). The LXX does not soften Jacob's self-assessment; it uses a strong Greek term that preserves the harshness of the Hebrew *ra'im*. Notably, the LXX adds a line not in the MT: it specifies that Jacob's years "have not reached the days of the years of my fathers *in which they sojourned*," adding the sojourning qualifier explicitly — **ἐν αἷς παρῴκησαν** (*en hais parōkēsan*). This matches the MT but slightly expands the explicit contrast with the patriarchal ages.

**The famine-economy passage (vv. 13–26):** The LXX renders the economic vocabulary with precision. For **וַיֶּאֱסֹף יוֹסֵף אֶת-כָּל-הַכֶּסֶף** (*vayyeesof Yosef et-kol-hakesef*, "Joseph collected all the money"), the LXX has **συνήγαγεν Ιωσηφ πᾶν τὸ ἀργύριον** — "Joseph gathered all the silver." The verb **συνήγαγεν** (*synēgagen*, "gathered, collected, assembled") carries the sense of systematic comprehensive collection, matching the Hebrew. For verse 21's **הֶעֱבִיר אֹתוֹ לֶעָרִים** (*he'evir oto le'arim*, "he moved them to the cities"), the LXX has **κατεδουλώσατο αὐτοὺς εἰς παῖδας** — "he enslaved them as servants/slaves." This is a significantly stronger rendering: where the MT says Joseph "relocated/transferred" the population (the verb *'avar* means to cross over, to move across), the LXX uses **κατεδουλώσατο** (*katedulōsato*) from **δουλόω** (*douloō*, "to enslave, to make a slave"). The LXX makes explicit what the MT leaves implicit: the relocation is a form of enslavement. This is a notable interpretive variant — the Greek translator was apparently willing to use the slavery vocabulary that the Hebrew narrator avoided.

**"Covenant-faithfulness and truth" (v. 29):** For **חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת** (*chesed ve-emet*), the LXX reads **ἐλεημοσύνην καὶ ἀλήθειαν** — "mercy/compassion and truth." The Greek **ἐλεημοσύνη** (*eleēmosynē*) is the LXX's standard rendering of *chesed*, emphasizing the merciful, compassionate dimension of the Hebrew term. (The same Greek word gives us "alms" in English — *eleēmosynē* came into church usage for charitable giving.) The LXX's choice highlights the gracious, undeserved quality of *chesed*, while the MT's term emphasizes covenant loyalty. Both translations are capturing real dimensions of the Hebrew word.

**The bed/staff variant (v. 31):** As discussed in the PNEUMA Meaning Note above, the MT reads **עַל-רֹאשׁ הַמִּטָּה** (*al-rosh ha-mittah*, "at the head of the bed"), vocalized as *mittah* (bed) by the Masoretes. The LXX reads **ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τῆς ῥάβδου αὐτοῦ** (*epi to akron tēs rhabdou autou*, "at the top of his staff"), translating the consonantal **מטה** as *matteh* (staff). This variant is visible in the Vaticanus text on pa_0048: the Greek word **ΡΑΒΔΟΥ** (rhabdou, "of the staff") appears clearly in the final line of the Genesis 47 section, confirming the LXX staff-reading in this specific manuscript. The Hebrews 11:21 citation follows the LXX precisely.

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 47 is structured in five distinct panels, each with its own moral register, moving from family narrative through political economy and back to intimate deathbed scene:

**Panel 1 — The Audience with Pharaoh (vv. 1–6):** Joseph stages the meeting carefully. He presents five brothers — not all twelve sons (perhaps the most presentable five?), or perhaps five as a round number. Pharaoh's response is generous: Goshen, the best of the land, and employment managing royal livestock. The scene is smooth, diplomatic, successful. The family is provisioned.

**Panel 2 — Jacob Meets Pharaoh (vv. 7–10):** The emotional and theological center of the chapter. Jacob blesses — twice. The patriarch of the covenant family stands before the god-king of the ancient world, and the blessing flows from the landless refugee toward the emperor. Jacob's three-verse speech in response to Pharaoh's question is a meditation on the sojourning life: 130 years, few, evil, insufficient by the standard of his fathers. It is the most honest speech in the chapter.

**Panel 3 — The Settlement (vv. 11–12):** Brief and warm. Joseph settles his family in "the best of the land." The phrase **רַמְסֵס** (*Rameses*) appears here — the same name that will appear in Exodus 1:11, where Israel is forced to build "store cities, Pithom and Rameses." The geography of settlement and the geography of later slavery are the same geography.

**Panel 4 — The Famine Economy (vv. 13–26):** The chapter's longest and morally densest section. Joseph systematically acquires money, livestock, land, and persons for Pharaoh. He institutes a permanent 20% tax. He exempts only the priests. The text is unblinking but uneditorializing. The people call him their savior. The statute is still in force "to this day." The narrator offers no verdict.

**Panel 5 — The Deathbed Oath (vv. 28–31):** The chapter closes in intimate family space. The famine economy recedes; we are back at the bedside of an old man and his son. Seventeen years have passed since the settlement — Jacob has lived longer in Egypt than any of his Egyptian years have felt. He is 147. He asks for the covenant gesture, the covenant formula, the covenant promise: bury me in Canaan. Joseph swears. Jacob bows in worship.

The structural irony is precise: the chapter that contains Genesis' most thoroughgoing account of imperial economic power — a state that owns everything, that has absorbed all money, all livestock, all land, all persons — ends with one old man and one son, and an oath about where a body will be buried. Against the total power of Pharaoh's Egypt, the covenant community asserts the quiet irreducible claim of the promise: *these bones belong to another land.*

---

Folio 28r (GEN_047_page_002.jpg) carries the remainder of the chapter (approximately vv. 20–31).
